original_abstract:
The rapid expansion of multi-sensor technology and the proliferation of massive datasets have exposed the constraints of traditional matrix-based data models. This paper provides a comprehensive signal processing perspective on the transition from two-way to multiway component analysis using higher-order tensors. By leveraging the mathematical framework of multilinear algebra, tensor decompositions offer a fundamental paradigm shift toward models that are essentially polynomial in nature. Unlike matrix methods, these multidimensional models ensure uniqueness under mild conditions and provide superior flexibility in incorporating constraints that reflect underlying data properties, such as sparsity, independence, and nonnegativity.

The authors review the algebraic foundations of tensor modeling, navigating from basic Canonical Polyadic (CPD) and Tucker decompositions to sophisticated schemes for multi-view data analysis and block-term models. The discussion demonstrates how tensor frameworks naturally generalize core signal processing tasks, including canonical correlation, subspace techniques, signal separation, and feature extraction. To address the "curse of dimensionality" inherent in big data, the paper explores computational strategies such as tensor networks (e.g., Tensor Train) and compressed sensing for efficient storage and manipulation. Supported by real-world case studies in fields ranging from brain-computer interfaces to hyperspectral imaging, the paper highlights how "tensorization" can reveal latent structures in data that remain hidden to standard flat-view matrix methods.
humanized_abstract:
Multisensor systems, sensors and large amount of data have brought the limit of the conventional matrix data representation method. From a signal processing point of view, this paper introduces the transition from traditional two-way component analysis to multiway component analysis by using higher-order tensors. By taking multilinear algebra as the underlying mathematical tool, several polynomial data representation models can be derived, which exhibit uniqueness under very mild conditions. More importantly, higher-order tensor models have the advantage of allowing for the inclusion of constraints on the underlying data, which often represent important data characteristics such as sparsity, independence, non-negativity etc.

Algebraic aspects of tensor modeling are reviewed by progressing from simple properties of the Canonical Polyadic Decomposition (CPD) and the Tucker decomposition towards multi-view analysis models and block-term models. It is shown through examples that tensor analysis can be naturally viewed as the generalization of important signal processing concepts such as canonical correlation analysis, sub-space methods, blind source separation and feature extraction. Means to cope with the large dimensionality of the data, a typical ill-conditioning inherent to large-dimensional data, are also considered. Tensor networks such as the examples of Tensor Train (TT) or compressed sensing are some of these means. Finally, several examples based on application fields such as brain-computer interfaces or hyperspectral imaging are given to illustrate the benefits derived from tensorization and therefore from making these hidden structures visible.
